title: Conserving an aged leather binding
materials: leather conservation cream (British Museum Leather Dressing or saddle soap equivalent)
bookbinding-thread-linen for minor rebacking if needed
sources: British Museum Leather Dressing formula is public domain; documented in various conservation literature. Draws on preservation guidance in H.J. Plenderleith, The Conservation of Antiquities and Works of Art (1956) which may still be in copyright — use structural description only. Synthesised from general leather conservation principles.
notes: Cleaning, conditioning, and preserving an old leather-covered binding. Covers: dry-dusting the boards and spine, testing a small area before treating the whole cover, applying leather dressing with a cloth (not a brush — brushes lift fibres), buffing with a clean cloth, using microcrystalline wax for stable protection. What NOT to do: no neatsfoot oil on oxidised leather (darkens and continues to acidify), no household furniture polish. When to stop and refer to a conservator (active red rot, torn joints, detached boards).
